sentences.text: Oh, fun.
Walter C Mason Jr-- W-A-L-T-E-R, C, M-A-S-O-N, J-R.
Muskegon, Michigan; December 15th, 1922.
Well, my parents were-- my father was of Norwegian and Danish descent.
My mother was of German descent.
We were brought up in a home with myself and one sister.
I have an older sister and an older brother.
Charlotte is my older sister, and John D was my brother.
We were Protestants.
We went to the Congregational Church.
Yes, we were.
Well, my father started his own business in 1929.
It was right after the depression, and he had a machine shop.
And my mother, to supplement the income, took in teachers for meals.
I had no real specific goals.
My mother always stressed education, and we wanted to look for college as an ultimate end to that education.
I didn't have any in my early years.
Later on, I was interested in chemistry and medicine, and decided that combination would work well if I took up pharmacy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, I have a bachelor of science degree in pharmacy.
I finished at the University of Michigan in 1947.
We were exposed to it.
I wouldn't say that any segment of our community was violently anti-Semitic or anti-racial.
And there were incidents of racial jokes and things of that nature that you were exposed to.
Not in my particular area, no.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes.
As a matter of fact, we had a Jewish family that moved in in my early teenage years-- moved in next door.
And he became one of my best friends.
Not with him, no.
I-- they moved away to Detroit, and I haven't heard from him since.
Yes I did.
I learned some of the Jewish customs and their holidays, and some of their dietary restrictions that I wasn't aware of before they moved in.
We had a excellent relationship.
Colemans were the name.
Robert was the young son, who was-- was approximately my age.
And we did a lot of things together.
They were a fairly well-to-do family as compared to ours, who I thought were still struggling.
So they were very generous in sharing some of the things that they did with us that-- that we weren't normally able to afford to do.
Not in our area, no.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No, I was not.
Well, at the time it-- I had entered college just before the Pearl Harbor incident.

Immediately after that period, while I knew most of us would go and most of us wanted to go, I joined the enlisted reserve corps, since they promised to keep us in college at least until our end of the term.
And then they'd call us.
I did go in in 1943.
We were informed by notice of 30 days, and then we took a bus to Camp Custer, Colorado-- or Camp Custer, Michigan.
And we went from there down to Texas to take our basic training in the infantry.
Well, we were concerned about our future.
A bunch of us were elected to go back to college after a completion of our basic training.
And we were concerned as to what type of education we might be afforded or allowed to take.
I can't recall any other specific conversations other than our concern of what was going to happen to us in the future.
Not particularly.
Everybody knew that we were going-- involved in a worldwide war, but nobody had any specific ideas of what promoted this war, other than Hitler's obvious expansionist activities.
Yes.
My mother would always say that it's not the German people, it's the German leaders that are producing this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, yes.
I was most impressed with the differences that I had seen from north to south, where they actually segregated the black population in restrooms, and eating establishments, and things of that nature.
I wasn't aware of that ever being a policy at any place that I had lived before.
Angry, I think.
I had had friends-- although the city that I lived in did not have a big black population-- those there were given equal opportunities in every instance that I was aware of.
Oh, yes.
We had a fairly large percentage of Jewish personnel in our unit.
We never had any problems.
They-- we mingled just without regard to racial or ethnic backgrounds.
Well, our basic training was in the infantry.
Because we were selected to go back to college, we were trained by some of the regular army.
And I think they felt that we were kinda a bunch of young smart individuals, and they were gonna see how much we could actually take.
So it was-- I thought we took a little rougher basic training than I'd normally be afforded.
I do not, no.
I didn't achieve any rank until after I joined the 120th Evacuation Hospital.
I was still a private, as were all the trainees at-- during basic training and while I attended New York University.
Well, as I say, I was attending New York University.
And we were ending a term.
And at about that time the invasion of Europe was beginning.
So they had decided to disband the Army Specialized Training Program, or ASTP as we called it at that time, and disperse the troops to active units.
My particular group was sent out to join the 104th infantry division in Camp Carson, Colorado.
At that particular time, going on a train to Camp Carson, I developed the mumps with about 40 other fellows.
And we were hospitalized.
It was the minute we got there.
By the time I got out of the hospital, they were making up a list of personnel who would be able to start this 104th-- or 120th Evacuation Hospital.

And because I had had some pharmacy training, I think my name was selected, and perhaps also because I wasn't available for active training at that time; they-- I'm sure the military commanders felt this was a good person to put on that list.
This was in 1944.
As I recall, it was in March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
This was a new unit.
When we got there, they had a-- just the bare minimum corps of cadre, and they were just beginning the table of organization to fill the cadre and then the routine trainees.
We were probably the second group to come in.
Yes, they was.
Colonel Williams was our commander at that time.
He was a lieutenant colonel-- medical officer.
I was told he was regular army.
Well, the regular army are those who make a career of the service.
And the reserve corps are those that act as-- don't make a career of it.
They basically are civilians who serve in time of need.
I never noticed it particularly.
I think during the war time, everybody worked together quite well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, we learned basic sanitation procedures.
The unit was designed to evacuate wounded from-- as a third echelon.
If I could describe the echelons of-- of medical treatment-- the battlefront went to a medical aid station.
Then they went to-- from there for more definitive clinic-- treatment-- to a clearing-- collecting station.
There they received some more treatment.
If they were well enough to go back to the lines, they went back from there.
If they required further medical treatment, they went back to a clearing company.
A clearing company segregated them, and then they went back to an evacuation hospital.
And if they were still unable to be returned to duty, they were flown back to station hospitals in England or in the States.
Well, we would receive them actually from the clearing company and give whatever definitive treatment might be appropriate in order to return them to the lines.
Or, as I say, if they required further rehabilitation, then they were transported back to the station or regional hospitals, either in England or the United States.
The executive officer, usually of the company.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We never exhibited any racial problems.
I think at one time there was a little prejudice toward some members of the Oklahoma group that came in later.
They-- we used to call them the Okies.
And for some reason or another we felt they were less educated than some of the others that were there.
But that was a short-term difference of opinion, and it blended well, and-- before the unit was sent overseas.
Oh, yes.
We had a large diversity of ethnic groups.
We had a reasonably large segment of Jewish.
There were Italians.
We had American Indians.
I'd say that we had a general mix of the population you might find in any major city in the United States.
We received extensive training both in the field and in the hospital setting.
Mostly we were trained to set up the tents-- show how we could operate the equipment in the field.
We were given-- oh, how do I want to say the-- imaginary patients that we treated on a evacuation process.
And we were tested by surgical inspector groups to determine our fitness to take care of the patients as they came in.
In the meantime, as a pharmacist, I worked in the dispensary and assisted in the sick call and the treatment of the minor ailments that we were able to treat within the company.
By the time we finished our training, I think we all felt quite competent in being able to perform our jobs.
We had 40 medical officers in our unit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We had section chiefs.
Each medical officer worked with a section.
Ours happened to be pharmacy, lab, and x-ray.
And we had a Captain Morton, who was our section chief for-- for the section.
And then we had one lieutenant who was the x-ray technician-- x-ray expert.
Well, I left on my 21st birthday [LAUGHS] on the It was a rather emotional setting.
I think we were all in a way anxious, and yet anticipating what might be forthcoming in the next few months.
But I think most of us were ready and willing to go.
I think we had some solace in the fact that we were-- had confidence in our training and would be able to perform our job well.
And at that station of the war, we felt that the allies were bound to predict victory.
The allied what?
We followed the movement of the troops to some degree to determine how far they were going each day as-- once the landing was made in France.
Of course, we followed the Italian and the African campaigns prior to that time.
But the big push was after the landing in France.
We knew that would be the last big stage, and if we were successful, why that should bring us a successful completion to the war.
No, not-- not-- I had one furlough, I think, before the unit went overseas.
But it was not just before, it was somewhere in the latter part of our training period.
I can't say that there were any real severe fears.
I think most of us had an inner fear that we might not come back and that something might happen to us.
But most of us were quite confident.
I think so.
I didn't dwell upon it nor give it that much thought.
Well, it was a nice crossing.
We crossed the ocean in about five days, I think.
It was a very luxurious liner, although our quarters were about four decks below the sea level.
[LAUGHS] It was so crowded that they had to section you off, and you couldn't go from one section to another without a special permission.
But we were able to get up on deck and see the ocean as we crossed.
And there again, you live in a rather confined quarter, and you are limited in your access to the other areas.
It wasn't quite a luxury crossing.
Yes.
At one period before we docked in Scotland we were told to sleep with our clothes on and keep a life preserver nearby.
And we found out later that the submarines were following the ship.
And the carried a rather large contingency.
So I'm sure that had they had the opportunity, they would have loved to have made that a target.
But fortunately, nothing ever transpired in that respect.
We landed in Lockerbie-- no, not-- I'm thinking of the plane-- the plane.
We landed in northern Scotland-- I can't remember the exact town.
And we were immediately entrained and went almost the length of the British islands down to Tenby, South Wales.
And we arrived there the day before Christmas, 1944.
By train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, we were pretty much put right on the train.
The only personnel that we had contact with were Red Cross gals who gave coffee, and donuts, and refreshments of one type or another.
No, we had not.
We were going to await further instructions at Tenby and continue some training at that point.
Many of the personnel were, depending upon their specialty, were sent out to regional hospitals in England to receive further training, for example, x-ray and lab.
Some of the nurses were sent to do ward work in some of the hospitals there.
Because my work had been in the dispensary, I and the other pharmacists remained there in the dispensary in the-- to service the medical-- immediate medical needs of our own unit.
That's correct.
Well, it's a little bit of a lonely one.
But in a way, it was a sense of adventure.
The inhabitants of the town were extremely nice to us.
And they celebrated Christmas more like we celebrate New Year's Eve.
It was a great day to go tavern to tavern and make friends.
And it was a joyous occasion in a way.
Well, we remained there for some weeks-- I can't remember the exact period of time.
And then we actually got word that we were to go across the channel to France.
And we didn't have any idea what we were going to face there.
We boarded some ships with the-- some Italian soldiers that they had gotten from the campaign, and took us overnight to the channel, and entered Le Havre harbor one morning.
I don't remember the exact date.
It was rough in some spots.
But it was done at nighttime, so I really didn't get a chance to see much of it.
I slept most of the way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In Le Havre, France.
And we were transported to-- I think the camp.
It was called Twenty Grand.
It was a processing camp in-- near Le Havre.
I was awe and struck-- awe-inspired by-- I shouldn't say inspired, but when I looked at Le Havre, it looked like a gravel pit.
All the buildings were pretty well bombed out, and you saw pieces of rubble all over the place.
And the war then became a little more personal.
You-- you could see the effects of the allied bombings, and there was hardly anything standing at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Prior to that, we had one furlough in London, and we were-- experienced one of the V bombs that the Germans had gone over.
We weren't hit, but you could hear the-- the whistling of the bomb.
And some were sent to bomb shelters at that time.
That was a little bit eerie, because you never had much warning about when those were gonna come.
Well, the invasion was well underway.
We were just playing catch-up at that time.
Only by radio, and there was no official communications to tell you.
There again, you're in a rather narrow segment.
You knew what your unit was doing, but not necessarily what everybody north, south, or east and west was doing.
I'm sorry?
We-- we went to several different staging areas, and it looked like we were still trying to catch up with the troops to see what assignment we might draw.
Finally, we ended up in a camp.
And we took a camp in Frankfurt am Main after we'd crossed the Rhine.
And we stayed there for a few days in a race track.
And we spent some time looking for souvenirs.
At one time they must have had a-- Olympic activities there, because we just found some old Olympic flags there.
And we found some medical equipment that had been abandoned by the Germans.
And we took some of that in and took what we could use to supplement our table of organization-- and equipment, I should say.
We were in Germany at that point.
Well, one night we had just recently been assigned to Patton's Third Army.
And in the middle of the night we were called out and said we're going to move out, destination unknown.
And we went some 120, 130 miles, I think, in one night, which was a long ways for a military unit to go in one day-- and got near enough to the front that it really frightened us.
I can remember yet that one of our trucks was told by a sentry, turn out those damn lights or I'm gonna shoot 'em out.
So we knew we were pretty close.
And we ended up in what turned out to be the city, Weimar, which was just outside the Buchenwald concentration camp.
And we were then told that we were going to work on liberating some prisoners.
No, we didn't.
They were told-- we were told that they were political prisoners and not American prisoners.
And that's all that we knew of it at that time.
Not the slightest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our company commander had said that there was a lot of work to be done and it was a mess up there.
And we didn't-- weren't aware of what it was until we actually walked in the gates.
And the first thing we saw was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] --bodies-- just cartloads of bodies there.
[SOB] [CRYING] There must have been three or four cartloads of bodies just cross-piled, just waiting outside of a crematorium.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We saw that and we saw the crematorium.
There was still bodies inside the crematorium-- and-- not live, they were shadows and skeletons of bodies.
And later we had a chance to go through some of the living quarters where the living prisoners were still there.
And they were in little cubicles about, oh, four to a section-- 8 by 12, or something like that.
It was so close that they had to lay on their sides.
And they had no pillows or anything.
All they had was a-- a metal pan, which they used for their food.
And it was my understanding they had to use that for their evacuation processes as well.
